sentences.text: So as I was saying, my mother was trying to tell us that she would stay the night away.
My grandmother was very upset and she said, really you shouldn't be doing that.
That's very dangerous, very dangerous.
Indeed, it was for everybody concerned.
So-- and she said, and do you know something?

As a matter of fact, I was going to make something for supper today and I already prepared something that you like so much-- I forget now what it is, I can't to tell you-- it's something that you like so much.
And so I was hoping that I wanted to surprise you.
So my mother said, all right.
I'll tell them I won't come that night, I'll come on another night.
And she didn't.
She told these ladies, as she promised.
She came home just because not to upset her mother.
She prepared whatever it was.
And it was very difficult to prepare anything, because by that time we really had nothing to eat practically.
And oh, I can't tell you-- frozen potatoes, frozen beetroot.
I mean, what she used to make out of that, my grandmother was a genius what she used to be able to cooking in bed because one couldn't-- one had nowhere to-- no cookers, nothing to cook.
So she would cook like rice and then put it inside the bed-- bedding and cook the rice, just boiling water over it.
And things like this.
And you know, whatever she prepared, it was some kind of a speciality for which she must have been racking her brain for a long, long time.
So my mother didn't want to upset her, she came.
So a few days later, the same story.
She says, but tonight I stay because I promised her.
Tonight please don't worry about me, I won't come.
So that was that.
She went off to work, we were in the ghetto.
Everything was more or less normal.
And suddenly toward the evening, rumors begin to spread, the rumors that the night they're closing down the ghetto.
All the gates, main and others, are going to be closed up completely and only those who come into the ghetto and go out of the ghetto who have a specific, specific insignia.

In other words, working, it's like a patch or badge or whatever-- more patch than a badge-- with the letter W, which was Wehrmacht, or R, which was Rostov [POLISH], which meant industry-- heavy industry.
Only those working for these institutions will be allowed in and out of the ghetto.
Everybody else is taken away and goes to [POLISH] and that's the extermination camp.
In other words, no other labor is necessary.
My mother worked for a civilian institution, German institution.
They didn't require any more Jewish labor there, obviously, from that moment on.
It was a terrible news and it started-- it was spreading very fast.
And so I realized that there must be a lot to it.
The ghetto is closed, and we saw, we heard all these goings-on.
We lived immediately behind the embankment, you would call it, the railway embankment, and there were gates here and there alongside this embankment.
And I thought, God Almighty, what is going to happen?
My mother doesn't have any of these-- neither of these two, rather.
She will obviously want to come back to the ghetto in the morning, and it's not easy to come back into the ghetto.
You have to join a group coming back from work night shift.
She would have to do that in order to get out of the ghetto with her group going to her work.
That is not necessary anymore.
That group either cannot get out or if they get out, they cannot come back again.
I knew that if she wants to get back into the ghetto, she will be taken away at the gate.
And I decided there and then that I'm going to escape from the ghetto and try to prevent her from coming.
I have to get to her in time that she does know.

Now, that was not very easy for money, from any aspect, but certainly to begin with because I knew that I must not utter a sound about my intentions to my grandparents because they would never allow me.
They would never let me go.
So that was in my decision, how to do it, how to overcome all the obstacles.
Everybody that night was going down to the cellar, where there was a particular part of the cellar made to a hiding place, a sort of under the floor boards and so on.
And it was detached a little bit from the other part of the cellar.
Of course, ludicrous.
If they had come and wanted to find with a dog, one dog would sniff it out.
But-- but one was, you know, one was so naive thinking, oh, nobody will find us here.
Nonsense, they would have done.
But this is where we all-- all the inhabitants of the house-- went down through these floorboards, down the ladder, into that particular hiding cellar, me-- myself included, of course.
And I thought I shall wait until such a time when everybody is fast asleep and then I'll try.
Hopefully, nobody will catch me because none of these people would have allowed me to go.
First of all, they'd be scared that if I'm caught, I'll spill the beans.
And secondly, they wouldn't allow me to go because I was a child, right?
So I didn't want to have any suspicions raised, so I changed.
I put my pajamas on and I had my slippers on and just a slight coat over me.
It was November.
And that's how we were downstairs.
And I pretended I was going to sleep.
Everybody was sort of slowly, slowly falling asleep.
It was quiet.
And I don't know what time it was, maybe it was one o'clock in the morning, maybe it was a little later.
I can't tell what time, I had-- we had-- I had nothing to tell the time by.

But when I knew and saw everybody is literally fast asleep, I decided this is my moment and I'm trying, going creeping up that ladder, that it shouldn't squeak, and then lifting the floorboards that they shouldn't make any-- the slightest noise.
And one was very alert.
People were very alert at the moment, at that time.
Your hearing was as sharp and your vision as sharp as all it ever can be.
And God knows I managed, and there I was outside.
I had to put the floorboards back and everything and the carpets on top, you know.
And I was out.
It was quite eerie, quite eerie-- quiet and cold, freezing, freezing cold.
And I think it was the 18th of November, 1942.
And I went out of the house, which was completely empty-- it was like a ghost house almost-- and into the street.
And the street was, of course, empty.
The moon was the size of-- completely white, shining.
I mean, the full moonlit night.
Of all nights, that night was it.
Moonlight night, full moon, around brand-- and virgin, virgin snow lying quite high.
Not snowing there and then, must have been snowing before.
So you can imagine, it was bright.
It was light through that and complete silence.
And here am I and you know, every step I thought the whole town to me because through the squeaking snow when you-- and freezing cold, freezing cold.
Here in my pajamas and a pair of slippers with a coat.
And I had to get over the embankment onto the other side.
I tried.
I climbed over the embankment and as I was on the embankment, there were two rail lines there running.
And suddenly, I heard shots-- shots all around me.
Well, of course, I was seen.
It's not surprising.
I was like a blob on the landscape.
I mean, suddenly this dark whatever, figure, and everything is white-- white moon, white snow, everything white and there am I.
So there were shots all around me.
I fell down and I was lying there.
And I didn't know whether anything hit me or not.
I had no idea.
There was all around shooting, shooting.
And I-- what is the word-- I mean, I rolled myself down to the other side of the embankment, rolled through the snow down.
I was like a snowman myself by then, all the snow clinging to me.
And I was lying there and thinking, now, what's going to be?
They must have seen me, otherwise they wouldn't be shooting.
Now, do they think I'm dead or do they-- have they seen me rolling down?
If they saw me rolling down, any moment somebody will be here and shoot me dead here on the spot.
So I was lying and I didn't dare to get up.
But nothing happened, nobody came-- nobody.
No shots, nobody, nothing.
So lying there, I don't know for how long, eventually I picked myself up and started walking.
Of course, I was on the other side of the track now, right?
And I walked through a street which even today seems like the longest street in the world.
It was a very long street, but perhaps not as long as it is in my memory.
On both sides of that street there were barracks, military barracks on both sides, and they were German military barracks at that time.
There was always a soldier standing duty on each side of the street.
And I thought that was the only street I could go, there was no other way.
And I thought, right, what do I do?
I mean, either I will go back, get myself back there, or I continue.
If I continue, of course, the soldiers see me certainly.
Where am I coming from?
I can only come from the ghetto, a child dressed the way I am.
Coming from there, there is nothing else but the ghetto-- I mean, you know, they've got me in the palm of their hand.
But I was walking, I was walking, I was walking quite fast.
I was freezing cold, walking fast.
And there is this soldier standing duty.
Of course you see, I don't look to him at all, walk right past him.
And I thought, now he's going to shoot me from the back.
But no, nothing-- not a sound, not a motion, not from this one, not from the other one on the other side.
Now, you can't-- you can't possibly assume that suddenly they went blind or deaf or something-- no.
But it was just one of those things, one of those miraculous escapes.
And also like I say, I call these people these sort of unsung heroes because this was-- these were two men who obviously knew who I was, there's no doubt about that.
They could have had me, they could have easily, there was no problem.
They did nothing, they let me go.
In other words, they allowed me to escape, right?
And that's the way I look at it.
I don't know who I-- I wish I could have known who they were.
I would have thanked them at some point later in my life, maybe I'd have visited them.
But I have no idea.
But there they were, ordinary soldiers standing guard.
And so I was on my way.
I walked and walked and walked.
And then, of course, the problem started that I don't know what time it was, but it was nearing early hours of the morning.
And my mother was quite a long way away from where I-- from where I was at the moment.
And I decided I will have to take a tram.
I can't walk through town the way I am because I'm, you now, I mean, a catch 21.
Can't walk like this.
And anyway, the curfew was on and so.
So I walked up to the terminal, tram terminal, which were not far away from there, I knew about it.
And I put myself into a tram which was going in the direction where my mother was.
And I thought, I'll sit.
At least I was inside something, not outside freezing.
And I'll wait and when the tram starts, I'll go.
We'll see what happens.
Maybe I'll manage, maybe I won't.
So there I was.
And then slowly, slowly, people started appearing and taking the tram.
First workman, workers, men-- mostly men-- non-Jews.
Of course, this was on the Aryan side, on the other side.
And they were boarding this tram, obviously, for the first tram to take them to work.
So it must have been six o'clock in the morning or 5:30 or something.
And I put myself in sort of a little corner and squeezed myself there and there I sat in my attire.
People were coming in, looking at me and looking the other way, leaning down.
Yeah.
The tram filled out almost to capacity.
I remember it was so full.
It usually was in Poland that it was overfull, it was overflowing, they were hanging out on the outside, you know?
And the conductor comes in and the driver, the train driver.
The conductor walks in, takes money from everyone, and here am I.
So you now, all these things are very well, but you don't envisage.
I mean, I did not think about it beforehand, how am I going to-- if I had thought about it, I might not have been able to do it.
Because logically speaking, you-- you are a dead duck.
It was impossible to get through all of that without being-- without being denounced or caught or whichever.
But here I was.
The conductor didn't see me.
He walked past, he didn't see me.
All the people around, nobody saw me.
They were doing, they were talking, they were doing their own thing, reading the paper.
I don't know, nobody saw me and there was I.
I got off where I wanted to get off.
Nobody touched me.
And I got to the place where my mother lives.
Now, it was so early in the morning that the main entrance door was still locked.
They had a habit like on the continent to lock at 10 o'clock in the evening and open at 7:00 in the morning.
It was obviously earlier than that because it was still locked.
I could not possibly ring that a bell or anything like that because they would have had a shock it's the Gestapo or something, you know.
So I had to start hiding before I could enter so that nobody again sees me and so on.
It was all terrible.
I waited until that and then I went up, must have been seven o'clock when I went up.
I rang the bell.
You can imagine it was a terrible upheaval there.
They really thought something, that some Gestapo was coming because of my mother being there and so forth.
When they saw me, they couldn't believe their eyes and of course, neither could my mother, and the state I was in.
I can't imagine.
I mean, I think I was an icicle dropping off of me all over the place.
And I told her the situation and what are we going to do now?
Now the question is that one can't go back-- we cannot go back to the ghetto.
What do we do?
Because if we go back to the ghetto, both of us are taken away.
What do we do?
We have to first of all go back to work.
My mother had to go to work so she took me along and went to work.
Now, here I am with her and I have nothing else to wear but what I had on.
And I was sitting there in the corner quietly.
Everybody said, oh, where is she from, why-- all these questions my mother had to answer.
And then walks in this tall German lady for whom my mother is sewing.
And she looks at me, who is this?
My mother said, this is my daughter.
I told you I have a daughter.
Oh.
Why-- why isn't she dressed?
What's she doing like this here?

So my mother told her, well, she came with me from the ghetto today-- she couldn't say that she'd stayed behind or anything-- she came with me from the ghetto [INAUDIBLE] and the situation is very difficult now and I don't know how we should get back and so forth.
So what do you mean she came like this, not dressed?
How can she walk like this?
You have to put something on this child and so on.
And so she was expressing some kind of sympathy.
And every now and again she would come in and look at me with the most pitiable expression, you now sort of-- and I was embarrassed to look at her.
At one point, my mother decided out of the blue she's going to ask her to take us in.
And she decided that when she has a fitting with her, she would do a fitting in a separate room so that nobody can hear and she would chance a question.
This was very difficult because she was German.
She did that and she asked this question.
And she, said could you possibly take us for just until this selection is over, until this quietens down a little bit?
And then I promise you, we will both go back to the ghetto, we won't stay.
It's just for three days, four days, two days, however long this lasts.
How can you even suggest such a thing to me, she said.
How can you suggest?
You know what that means?
That means it's a death penalty.
How could I?
I can't possibly and where would I put you?
You know.
But my mother knew that she lived alone.
She had no family, she lived alone.
She had a beautiful apartment, of course an ex-Jewish apartment, in a beautiful part of town which was confiscated for.
And this was an SS and police district only, and the house belonged to the SS and police, and she had an apartment in it.
She had an apartment there because at some point before, she had a friend, what we would call a boyfriend, if you like, who was a very high-ranking Gestapo man.
And he got that apartment.
This was-- what is the word-- I mean, this was not given to him, but it was-- I'm missing the word.
He had the right to choose whatever he liked in that particular time-- part of [INAUDIBLE].
And he chose this apartment and it was in the SS and police district.
And when they were together, she was there, too-- with him.
At some point, he was sent away from Lwow to Holland.
And from the way we understood it, he was heading a Jewish concentration camp in Holland.
But that was his allocation there, and so he had to leave almost on the spur of the moment and he left her in that apartment.
And he said, you might as well stay here.
Why would you look for anything else?
This is mine, you stay here.
And she stayed.
And he was away for quite some time.
And we knew that she is living on her own, she has nobody, nobody.
And she was very much a loner.
She didn't socialize very much.
She had a habit which was until the end of her days a most-- most incredible habit.
In fact, she ate spoonfuls-- spoonfuls-- of garlic every day, every day.
And of course, she reeked to high heaven and it was very difficult to be around her, even at work.
She had-- she was totally isolated, she couldn't care less.
She said, to my mind this is the one medicine that-- that keeps you healthy and this is what I'm eating.
If somebody doesn't like it, they don't need to be near me.
And she worked like that on this premise and she couldn't care less.
So people were a little further away, so they were-- and to a boyfriend, she didn't need the boyfriend.
He was in Holland, that was fine.
She ate her garlic and she didn't socialize.
And she was always terribly tired.
She told my mother, oh, I'm so tired.
I come home, I'm so tired, exhausted.
I hate working in the office.
I hate office work, I hate what I'm doing.
But I have to do it because I-- I must.
We all must do our bit for the war, so I'm must.
But I hate the office.
I get so tired.
I come home, I collapse into bed.
So my mother would say, well, don't you go out, don't you see anybody?
No, I don't see anybody.
I don't go out.
All I want is to go have my bath and go to bed.
So you know, I mean, the conditions were absolutely primal there for us and marvelous.
But for her to accept such a thing was another matter.
And so when my mother suggested it, she was shocked.
She was utterly, utterly shocked at first.
And then she kept coming in and looking at me, looking at me over and over again and again and again.
And my mother, again, had another fitting with her, I need another fitting.
Have you considered?
Please would you do that?
I mean, look at this child.
You know, what am I going to do with her and the ghetto, we go back, they take her away.
And so on.
And the third time, she said, all right, I'll take you.
I'll take you for these few days, but you have to be very careful how you follow me.
At night we will leave-- of course, it was very early evening, it was November.
So by the time five o'clock when they finished work, it was dark.
You will see me walking with so-and-so and so-and-so.
We walk always home together and then part-- we part ways at such and such a street.
They go that way, I go my way.
But you must follow at a long, long distance.
Don't be anywhere near us because they know you.
That was so.
Of course, it was very difficult also for us to get away because of the other people.
Everybody was asking, what are you going to do?
How are you going to go back?
How are you going to-- it was awfully difficult not to say anything to these people, to the other co-workers, to detach ourselves.
And my mother, I mean, awful.
When we did that, we had to.
We did that and we hid in some place which she advised to do.
And she said, from there you can see me and when I walk then within about a minute, start following.
That's what we did, we followed her and then we went to her.
She lived on the third floor.

There were nothing but high, high-ranking officers living in that house, plus immediately underneath her was the Ukrainian chief of police, the chief of the militia Ukrainian was living underneath her.
And to get into her apartment was another story because to walk up the three flights of stairs not to be noticed or to go and live not to be noticed was another story.
